decisions.decision: How SDK-backed provider registrations obtain Azure clients
decisions.choice: `AddAzure*Sdk()` registrations will adapt Azure SDK clients that the host already registered in DI, instead of constructing credentials or Azure clients inside this library.
decisions.rationale: This keeps provider selection explicit, reuses `Microsoft.Extensions.Azure` lifetime/configuration behavior, avoids duplicating credential bootstrapping, and lets SDK-backed and in-memory modes share the same focused factory seam.

decisions.decision: How focused resource factories expose capability without mirroring the Azure SDK
decisions.choice: Keep each public factory resource-specific and pair it with a small set of library-owned companion abstractions (for example sender/processor/admin, blob container/blob client, and secret store) instead of returning raw Azure SDK clients from the seam.
decisions.rationale: This preserves the explicit backend-swapping seam from D002/D004, keeps the contracts sized to M001 scenarios, and gives later in-memory slices stable surfaces to implement without chasing full SDK parity.

decisions.decision: How early in-memory Blob and Key Vault seams behave before their dedicated slices
decisions.choice: Ship Blob and Key Vault registrations with truthful shared-state basic read/write behavior now, using singleton InMemoryBlobState and InMemoryKeyVaultState roots instead of registration-only stubs.
decisions.rationale: S01 tests already prove upload/download/exists for blobs and set/get with generated versions for secrets, so downstream slices can build on working in-memory behavior rather than replacing placeholder adapters.

decisions.decision: How AddAzure*Sdk registrations obtain required Azure SDK clients
decisions.choice: Resolve required Azure SDK clients lazily through DI activation lambdas when the focused factory is first resolved, and throw resource-specific actionable InvalidOperationException messages if the host did not register the needed client types.
decisions.rationale: Service Bus, Blob, and Key Vault registration tests prove the same focused seam can stay explicit while surfacing missing ServiceBusClient, ServiceBusAdministrationClient, BlobServiceClient, and SecretClient dependencies at factory resolution time instead of failing silently or constructing clients internally.

decisions.decision: How same-resource SDK vs in-memory registrations compose
decisions.choice: Use per-resource registration markers together with TryAddSingleton so repeated same-mode registrations are idempotent, while registering both SDK and in-memory backends for the same resource fails fast with actionable guidance.
decisions.rationale: The mixed-provider composition tests show Service Bus, Blob, and Key Vault can each choose their backend independently in one IServiceCollection, while same-resource conflicts still throw deterministic messages that name both competing AddAzure* methods.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: Service Bus, Blob, Key Vault, and mixed-composition tests prove each resource can choose SDK or in-memory registration independently through explicit AddAzure* methods in one IServiceCollection.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: BlobProviderRegistrationTests prove the in-memory blob seam can upload, download, and check existence through IAzureBlobFactory with content type preservation and no external infrastructure.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: AzureServiceBusSdkFactory, AzureBlobSdkFactory, and AzureKeyVaultSdkFactory adapt DI-registered official Azure clients behind the same focused seams, and the registration tests prove those SDK-backed factories resolve correctly.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: KeyVaultProviderRegistrationTests prove the in-memory secret store can SetSecretAsync and GetSecretAsync through IAzureKeyVaultFactory without external infrastructure, including missing-secret null behavior and generated versions.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: BlobProviderRegistrationTests prove the in-memory blob seam can upload, download, and check existence through IAzureBlobFactory with content type preservation and no external infrastructure.

decisions.decision: Where topology-aware in-memory Service Bus ingress lives in M001/S02
decisions.choice: Keep `IAzureServiceBusFactory` and the processor seam unchanged, and implement truthful queue/topic ingress routing inside `InMemoryServiceBusState`, with dedicated behavior tests proving queue storage, topic-to-subscription fan-out, and invalid-topology diagnostics.
decisions.rationale: S01 already established the focused Service Bus seam and shared state root. Putting ingress behavior in the state keeps the sender thin, makes topology creation meaningful, preserves the explicit S03 processor boundary, and gives tests a single inspection surface for pending-message proof without widening the public API prematurely.

decisions.decision: How in-memory Service Bus ingress handles undeclared entity paths
decisions.choice: InMemoryServiceBusState now requires queue or topic topology to be declared before enqueueing and throws an actionable InvalidOperationException that points callers to the administration creation methods.
decisions.rationale: This prevents silent entity creation, keeps queue ingress observable through explicit state assertions, and gives later slices a truthful topology boundary to build processor behavior on.

decisions.decision: How in-memory Service Bus topic publishes are represented in pending state
decisions.choice: Publish sends fan out cloned envelopes into declared `<topic>/Subscriptions/<subscription>` pending queues and never enqueue against the topic name itself.
decisions.rationale: This matches the observable topology the later processor slice will consume, preserves per-subscription message isolation, and prevents the in-memory sender from inventing a topic-local pending queue that does not exist in real Service Bus processing semantics.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: S02 added dedicated in-memory Service Bus ingress tests and topology-aware state behavior proving a test can create queues/topics/subscriptions in memory, send or publish messages, and observe preserved pending envelopes on the correct queue or `<topic>/Subscriptions/<subscription>` paths entirely inside `dotnet test` with no external infrastructure.

decisions.decision: Where processor lifecycle observability lives for in-memory Service Bus
decisions.choice: Keep completed, dead-lettered, errored, and requeued in-memory Service Bus processor outcomes in `InMemoryServiceBusState`, and keep `InMemoryServiceBusProcessor` thin over those state-owned transition APIs.
decisions.rationale: S02 already centralized topology and pending envelopes in shared state. Keeping lifecycle transitions in the same state preserves canonical queue/subscription observability, avoids widening `IAzureServiceBusFactory`, and lets tests assert durable completion/dead-letter/error outcomes even when handlers fail or no `ProcessErrorAsync` callback is configured.

decisions.decision: How in-memory processor runs consume pending envelopes
decisions.choice: Drain the current pending batch synchronously and use state-owned completed/dead-lettered/errored outcome stores keyed by queue or canonical subscription entity path.
decisions.rationale: S03 tests prove deterministic processor behavior is easier to assert when StartProcessingAsync drains a snapshot of the pending buffer, routes explicit settlement back through InMemoryServiceBusState, and leaves later ingress or requeued work for the next run instead of interleaving state mutations mid-loop.

decisions.decision: How in-memory processor execution reports failures
decisions.choice: Treat handler exceptions and invalid second-settlement attempts as terminal errored outcomes, preserve any first successful settlement, and surface the failure through ProcessErrorAsync when configured.
decisions.rationale: The processor tests prove useful failure visibility comes from recording the original envelope plus exception in GetErroredMessages(entityPath) without silently requeueing it, while dead-letter-first or complete-first transitions remain deterministic and inspectable for tests.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: S03 added queue and subscription processor execution tests that prove CompleteMessageAsync and DeadLetterMessageAsync move stored envelopes into inspectable completed and dead-lettered outcome buckets on declared entity paths, with broader ServiceBus and full-solution test runs passing inside dotnet test.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: S03 proves tests can inspect pending, completed, dead-lettered, and errored outcomes through InMemoryServiceBusState after processor execution, including actionable diagnostics for undeclared topology and invalid settlement ordering, all verified by the processor-focused suite and broader regressions.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: The slice extends the in-memory Service Bus harness with GetCompletedMessages, GetDeadLetteredMessages, GetErroredMessages, and stable envelope/outcome records so tests can assert processor behavior beyond the official SDK surface without external infrastructure.

decisions.decision: How S04 handles already-delivered Key Vault basics
decisions.choice: Treat S04 as a verification-and-closeout slice that adds dedicated Key Vault behavior proof without widening the public Key Vault seam.
decisions.rationale: S01 already implemented and validated the roadmap demo for Key Vault basics. Rebuilding the set/get path would duplicate working behavior and risk unnecessary API churn, so S04 should close the traceability gap by extracting focused Key Vault behavior tests, preserving the infrastructure-free test loop, and keeping observability in the existing shared state surface.

decisions.decision: Where Key Vault behavioral proof lives
decisions.choice: Keep runtime behavior proof in a dedicated in-memory Key Vault suite and keep Key Vault registration tests DI-only.
decisions.rationale: S04 added InMemoryKeyVaultBehaviorTests as the authoritative proof surface for AddAzureKeyVaultInMemory() and IAzureKeyVaultFactory set/get behavior, including missing-secret null lookups, case-insensitive overwrite/latest-version semantics, and fail-fast validation, while KeyVaultProviderRegistrationTests stays focused on backend resolution, shared-state reuse, and actionable misconfiguration diagnostics.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: S04's dedicated InMemoryKeyVaultBehaviorTests now prove SetSecretAsync/GetSecretAsync through AddAzureKeyVaultInMemory() and IAzureKeyVaultFactory with no external infrastructure, including missing-secret null behavior, case-insensitive overwrite/latest-version semantics, shared-state inspection, and fail-fast invalid-input checks; the focused Key Vault suite, the broader Key Vault filter, and the full solution all passed via dotnet test.

decisions.decision: How S05 handles already-delivered Blob basics
decisions.choice: Treat S05 as a verification-and-closeout slice that adds dedicated Blob behavior proof without widening the public Blob seam.
decisions.rationale: S01 already implemented and validated the roadmap demo for Blob basics through the in-memory factory and shared state. Rebuilding the write/read path would duplicate working behavior and risk unnecessary API churn, so S05 should close the traceability gap by extracting focused Blob behavior tests, keeping Blob registration tests DI-only, and preserving the infrastructure-free `dotnet test` loop.

decisions.decision: Where Blob behavioral proof lives
decisions.choice: Keep runtime behavior proof in a dedicated in-memory Blob suite and keep Blob registration tests DI-only.
decisions.rationale: S05 added InMemoryBlobBehaviorTests as the authoritative proof surface for AddAzureBlobInMemory() and IAzureBlobFactory blob basics, including missing-blob null/false behavior, overwrite semantics, case-insensitive identity, cloned snapshots, and fail-fast validation, while BlobProviderRegistrationTests stays focused on backend resolution and diagnostics.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: S05's dedicated InMemoryBlobBehaviorTests now prove upload/download/exists through AddAzureBlobInMemory() and IAzureBlobFactory with missing-blob false/null behavior, overwrite conflict vs replacement semantics, case-insensitive container/blob lookup, cloned content snapshots, GetContainer namespace establishment, and all focused/blob/full-solution dotnet test runs passing without external infrastructure.

decisions.choice: validated
decisions.rationale: With S05 closing the final Blob proof gap, the focused Blob suite, Blob-filtered run, and full ./Azure.InMemory.sln test run all pass sequentially inside dotnet test with no Azure account, Docker, or external infrastructure, demonstrating the supported M001 scenarios execute fully in-process.

decisions.decision: Whether Azure Functions Blob Trigger work belongs in M002
decisions.choice: Keep Blob Trigger emulation deferred out of M002 and do not create a roadmap slice for it.
decisions.rationale: Blob Trigger work would drag runtime and host emulation concerns into a milestone that is supposed to stay focused on Service Bus fidelity and internal-ready packaging.

decisions.decision: What is the first M002 fidelity target
decisions.choice: Focus M002 Service Bus depth on observable redelivery, delivery count progression, and automatic dead-letter at the max-delivery threshold.
decisions.rationale: This is the narrowest high-value realism upgrade after M001 because it extends the existing deterministic processor/state model, catches meaningful integration mistakes, and avoids drifting into broad Azure Service Bus parity work like sessions, scheduling, or host simulation.

decisions.decision: Whether Azure Functions Blob Trigger work belongs in M002
decisions.choice: Keep Blob Trigger emulation deferred out of M002 and do not create a roadmap slice for it.
decisions.rationale: Blob Trigger work would drag runtime and host emulation concerns into a milestone that is supposed to stay focused on Service Bus fidelity and internal-ready packaging.

decisions.decision: What counts as internal-ready packaging proof for M002
decisions.choice: Treat internal-ready packaging as a real producer-to-package-to-consumer flow: successful `dotnet pack`, package-facing docs/examples, and fresh consumer restore/use proof through a local NuGet path rather than project references.
decisions.rationale: The milestone context requires actual package consumption from outside the producing project boundary, so metadata cleanup alone would be insufficient and misleading.

decisions.decision: How M002/S01 should model redelivery fidelity
decisions.choice: Model redelivery as state-owned requeue across repeated explicit `StartProcessingAsync()` runs, with delivery-count and max-delivery metadata kept in `InMemoryServiceBusState`, instead of background retry loops or new public runtime abstractions.
decisions.rationale: This preserves the deterministic in-process `dotnet test` model from M001, fits the existing queue and canonical subscription entity-path lifecycle, avoids a seam rewrite, and keeps failure diagnosis inspectable through the current test harness.

decisions.decision: How queue redelivery exposes attempt state and failure observability in M002/S01/T03
decisions.choice: Expose DeliveryCount on AzureServiceBusReceivedMessageContext, keep delivery-count/max-delivery metadata on the state-owned envelope lifecycle, and record each failed queue attempt in the errored bucket even when the message is requeued or auto-dead-lettered.
decisions.rationale: Queue redelivery needs handlers and tests to observe which attempt is being processed without adding a new abstraction, while downstream assertions still need durable proof of each failure attempt and the final pending/completed/dead-letter disposition. Keeping the counts on the envelope lifecycle and preserving errored outcomes per failed attempt fits the existing deterministic processor seam and gives T04 a stable subscription model to extend.

decisions.decision: How M002/S01 redelivery fidelity applies to topic subscriptions
decisions.choice: Reuse the same state-owned retry/dead-letter lifecycle on canonical `<topic>/Subscriptions/<subscription>` entity paths so each subscription clone advances `DeliveryCount`, requeues, and exhausts `MaxDeliveryCount` independently.
decisions.rationale: The subscription redelivery tests show topic fan-out already produces per-subscription clones, so extending the existing queue redelivery bookkeeping to canonical subscription paths preserves the explicit seam, keeps retries deterministic per `StartProcessingAsync()` run, and prevents one failing subscription from mutating or exhausting its siblings.

decisions.decision: How S02 should package docs for internal-ready consumption
decisions.choice: Use the root `README.md` as the single authoritative package-facing quickstart and pack that file into the `.nupkg`, while deferring any committed sample consumer project to S03.
decisions.rationale: A single source of truth avoids drift between repo docs and package docs, keeps S02 narrowly focused on internal-ready packaging, and leaves the fresh outside-the-producer consumer proof to S03 where local NuGet restore/use is the actual acceptance target.

decisions.decision: How Azure.InMemory declares its baseline package version in S02
decisions.choice: Keep an explicit baseline package version of 1.0.0 in the library project and let downstream proof override it intentionally via pack properties when needed.
decisions.rationale: S02 needs a deterministic internal package artifact name for inspection and repeatable local pack proof, while S03 still needs a clean way to publish distinct consumer-proof builds without relying on NuGet cache luck or implicit default versioning.

decisions.decision: How S03 should prove package consumption from inside this repo without false producer coupling
decisions.choice: Use a standalone package-only consumer xUnit project under `samples/`, keep it out of `Azure.InMemory.sln`, disable Central Package Management locally in that project, and verify it through a local-feed pack/restore/test script with an isolated consumer package cache.
decisions.rationale: This keeps the proof outside the producer project boundary, prevents hidden solution/project-reference or repo-root CPM coupling, and makes the consumer evidence come from the same `.nupkg` + NuGet restore path another internal team would actually use.

decisions.decision: How S03 verifies external package consumption without stale-cache false positives
decisions.choice: Use a single verifier that repacks Azure.InMemory, recreates the sample-local consumer package cache, restores through the sample-local NuGet.Config, runs the focused external-consumer test suite, and then reruns the producer solution tests.
decisions.rationale: The S03 proof must come from the current .nupkg and a real PackageReference restore path rather than hidden global caches or project coupling. Repacking first and isolating the consumer cache makes same-version package verification truthful, while rerunning the producer solution keeps the consumer proof from masking regressions inside the main repo.
